message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining today. How are you doing? I hope you're having a great day so far.
Hi! I'm doing well, thank you—just a bit of pre-interview jitters, but excited to chat. How about you? Hope your day's going smoothly too!

Oh, I love that energy! A few jitters are totally normal—it means you care. And thank you, my day’s going well, especially now that I get to meet candidates like you. 

Before we dive in, I’d love to know: What’s something you’re looking forward to in this conversation or the opportunity ahead?

Oh, that’s a great question! I’m really looking forward to diving into the technical details of how Zephlen is building its agentic systems—especially the orchestration layers and how you handle edge cases in production. From my experience at LojistikZeka, I’ve seen how tricky it can be to make these systems reliable, so I’m curious about your approach.

Also, I’m excited to share some of the work I’ve done with autonomous agents, like the supply chain rerouting system I built. It’s always fun to geek out over this stuff!

I love your enthusiasm for the work—it’s clear you’re passionate about what you do! We’ll definitely have time to explore those technical interests later in the process, but since this is our first conversation, I’d love to get to know *you* a little better beyond the tech. 

For example, I noticed you mentioned your experience at LojistikZeka. That sounds like a challenging environment! Could you share a bit about what it was like working there? What’s something—good or bad—that stood out to you about the culture or the way the team operated?

Oh, absolutely! LojistikZeka was a really interesting place to work—especially the culture. On one hand, it was super fast-paced, almost chaotic at times, which I actually thrived in. The team was small but scrappy, and we had to move quickly to keep up with the demands of the supply chain industry. I loved that we were encouraged to experiment, even if it meant breaking things occasionally.

That said, it wasn’t all smooth sailing. The culture could be a bit *too* reactive—like, we’d often pivot to the latest crisis without always having a long-term plan. For example, when we first deployed the autonomous supply chain agent, there was a lot of pressure to get it live ASAP, which led to some technical debt we had to clean up later. But hey, that’s startups for you, right? You learn to adapt.

I’d say the biggest standout was how much ownership everyone had. Even as an individual contributor, I felt like my work directly impacted the company’s bottom line, which was really motivating. Though, looking back, I do wish we’d had a bit more structure around documentation—sometimes knowledge got siloed, and that made onboarding new team members a challenge.

That’s such a great reflection—it sounds like you learned a ton from that experience, both about what energizes you and what you’d want to improve in your next role. Ownership is such a powerful motivator, and it’s clear you thrive when you feel that direct impact.

Speaking of ownership and collaboration, I’d love to hear about a time when you had to navigate a tricky interpersonal dynamic—maybe pushing back on a decision or managing feedback with someone who saw things differently. 

For example, during the migration from LangChain to your custom orchestration engine at LojistikZeka, you likely faced resistance from stakeholders worried about disruption. Can you walk me through a moment like that? How did you balance your technical conviction with their concerns, and what did you learn about aligning people who might be coming from very different perspectives?

Oh, that’s a great example—though, actually, the migration from LangChain wasn’t as contentious as you might think. The team was pretty open to it because the existing pipeline was so brittle. But I *did* have a situation where I had to push back on a stakeholder’s request to rush the deployment of a new feature for the supply chain agent.

The sales team wanted to add real-time carrier API integrations to the agent’s toolset *before* we’d fully stress-tested the existing workflows. They were convinced it would close a big deal, but I knew it could destabilize the system if we didn’t properly handle rate limits and error cases. I remember one heated meeting where I had to explain that while I understood the business pressure, deploying half-baked tooling would cost us more in the long run—like the time we had to roll back a feature that caused a 20% spike in failed rerouting attempts.

I ended up proposing a compromise: we’d build a minimal version of the integration with strict safeguards and monitor it closely in a staging environment. That way, sales could demo it to the client, but we wouldn’t risk breaking production. It worked out—we caught a critical edge case during testing that would’ve caused major issues, and the client was still impressed with the progress.

Looking back, I think the key was framing my pushback in terms of *shared goals*—like, "I want this deal to succeed too, but here’s how we can do it without jeopardizing the system." It’s easy to get defensive when someone questions your work, but focusing on the bigger picture helped keep the conversation productive. Though, honestly, I still cringe thinking about how close we came to deploying that feature too early!

That’s such a powerful example—it really highlights how you think about collaboration and risk. I love how you turned a potential conflict into a win-win by focusing on shared goals and proposing a practical compromise. It’s clear you don’t just think about the technical side of things; you’re really intentional about how decisions impact the team and the business as a whole.

You also mentioned something interesting about how close you came to deploying that feature too early. That makes me curious: how do you typically approach situations where there’s uncertainty or pressure to move quickly? For example, have you ever had to make a tough call where you didn’t have all the data you needed, or where the stakes felt particularly high? How did you navigate that?

Oh man, that’s a great question. There was this one time at LojistikZeka where we had to decide whether to switch from OpenAI’s GPT-4 to Anthropic’s Claude 3 for our supply chain agent. The pressure was on because our token costs were skyrocketing, and Claude promised better efficiency—but we didn’t have time for a full benchmark against our golden dataset.

I remember the CTO wanted to just flip the switch and monitor in production, but I pushed back hard. We ended up running a quick A/B test in staging with a subset of historical queries, and within 48 hours, we found that Claude was actually *worse* at handling our edge cases—like parsing carrier API responses with missing fields. It would’ve been a disaster if we’d deployed it blindly.

In the end, we stuck with GPT-4 but optimized our prompts to reduce token usage. It wasn’t the perfect solution, but it bought us time to properly evaluate Claude later. The lesson? Even when you’re under pressure, you can’t skip the basics—like testing. Though, looking back, I wish I’d been more vocal earlier about the risks of switching models without proper validation. Hindsight’s 20/20, right?

(Though, funnily enough, we *did* end up switching to Claude a few months later after they released a new version—so it all worked out in the end!)

That’s such a fantastic example—it really shows how you balance pragmatism with conviction. I love how you pushed back *and* found a way to test the waters without fully committing. It’s clear you don’t shy away from uncertainty, but you also don’t let pressure force you into reckless decisions. 

You also mentioned something that stuck with me: the idea that hindsight is 20/20, and that you wish you’d been more vocal earlier about the risks. That kind of self-reflection is so valuable. 

I’d love to explore that a little more. Can you think of a time when something didn’t go as planned—maybe a project, a decision, or even a collaboration—and you realized afterward that you’d grown from the experience in an unexpected way? How did that moment shape how you approach challenges now?

Oh, absolutely. There was this one project early on at LojistikZeka where I was *convinced* I could build a self-healing agent that would automatically retry failed API calls without human intervention. I was so sure it would work that I didn’t even bother setting up proper alerts for when things went wrong. Spoiler: it *did* go wrong.

The agent ended up in this infinite retry loop with a carrier’s API during a major outage, and because I hadn’t built in any circuit breakers, it burned through our rate limits in hours. We had to scramble to manually intervene, and it cost us a ton in API fees and overtime. The worst part? The CTO had actually flagged this risk in a design review, but I brushed it off because I was so confident in my solution.

After that, I started treating "overconfidence" as its own failure mode. Now, I force myself to write down *all* the ways a system could fail before I even start coding—even the ones that seem ridiculous. It’s made me a lot more thorough, and honestly, it’s saved me from a few near-misses since then. Though, I’ll admit, I still catch myself getting too attached to my own ideas sometimes. Old habits die hard, right?

(Though, in my defense, the self-healing agent *did* work eventually—just not the way I originally built it!)
